perturbation.original_text: Such Confidential Information will be disclosed for the P urpose and for no other purpose in accordance with the following understandings: 1. Confidentiality Obligations . Each party shall maintain in confidence Confidential Information obtained from the other and use such Confidential Information only for the P urpose. Such Confidential Information shall be identified in writing and marked ''Confidential '' by the disclosing party or, if first presented orally or visually, shall be reduced to writing, the writing marked ''Confidential'' by the disclosing party and delivered to the receiving party within twenty (20) days of the oral or visual disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Such Confidential Information will be disclosed for the P urpose and for no other purpose in accordance with the following understandings: 1. Confidentiality Obligations . Each party shall maintain in confidence Confidential Information obtained from the other and use such Confidential Information only for the P urpose. Such Confidential Information shall be identified in writing and marked ''Confidential '' by the disclosing party or, if first presented orally or visually, shall be reduced to writing, the writing marked ''Confidential'' by the disclosing party and delivered to the receiving party within sixty (60) days of the oral or visual disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Extended the deadline for marking and delivering confidential information from 20 to 60 days, potentially exceeding reasonable timeframes and causing disputes over what was considered confidential at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification extends the allowable timeframe for designating information as 'Confidential,' potentially undermining trade secret protection. Under the UTSA, reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are required for trade secret status. A 60-day delay in marking orally disclosed information as confidential could be deemed unreasonable, jeopardizing its protection as a trade secret if challenged.

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the deadline for marking confidential information from 20 to 60 days. The Law Explanation suggests that this extension could be deemed unreasonable under the UTSA, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection due to a failure to make reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the potential for disputes arising from this extended timeframe. Although specific timelines aren't detailed in the provided snippets, the general legal principle of 'reasonable efforts' implies a need for timely action, which the 60-day period may undermine.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Return of Confidential Information. All written, printed, electronically stored or other tangible documents, samples and materials submitted by one party to the other hereunder, and all copies thereof remaining in the receiving party’s possession, shall promptly be returned to the disclosing party and electronic media erased upon request or upon termination of this Agreement, except that a copy of each may be retained by the receiving party under appropriate security.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Return of Confidential Information. All written, printed, electronically stored or other tangible documents, samples and materials submitted by one party to the other hereunder, and all copies thereof remaining in the receiving party’s possession, shall be returned to the disclosing party within 180 days of request or upon termination of this Agreement, except that a copy of each may be retained by the receiving party under appropriate security.
perturbation.explanation: Changed 'promptly' to 'within 180 days' for the return of confidential information, potentially violating state trade secret laws that require reasonable measures to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90(2)(a) defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. Delaying the return of confidential information for 180 days could be construed as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect its secrecy, thereby jeopardizing its status as a trade secret under Wisconsin law, which governs this agreement.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90 (2022) — Uniform trade secrets act. :: 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2023 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2022 Wis. Stat. & Ann. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations Chapter 134 - Miscellaneous trade regulations. 134.90 - Uniform trade secrets act. Universal Citation: WI Stat § 134.90 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 134.90 Uniform trade secrets act. (1) Definitions. In this section: (a) “Improper means" includes espionage, theft, bribery, misrepresentation and breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy. (b) “Readily ascertainable" information does not include information accessible through a license agreement or by an employee under a confidentiality agreement with his or her employer. (c) “Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process to which all of the following apply: 1. The information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. 2. The information is the subject of efforts to maintain its secrecy that are reasonable under the circumstances. (2) Misappropriation. No person, including the state, may misappropriate or threaten to misappropriate a trade secret by doing any of the following: (a) Acquiring the trade secret of another by means which the person knows or has reason to know constitute improper means. (b) Disclosing or using without express or implied consent a trade secret of another if the person did any of the following: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'promptly' with 'within 180 days' for returning confidential information. Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90(2)(a), as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, defines a trade secret as information subject to 'reasonable' efforts to maintain secrecy. Allowing a 180-day delay could be deemed an unreasonable measure that jeopardizes the trade secret status, creating a contradiction by not adhering to the 'reasonable' standard required to protect trade secrets under Wisconsin law.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Term; Termination. Either party may at any time upon written notice terminate this Agreement; otherwise participation by the parties in the review or evaluation shall end, and this Agreement shall terminate ten (10) years from the Effective D ate.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Term; Termination. Either party may at any time upon written notice terminate this Agreement; otherwise participation by the parties in the review or evaluation shall end, and this Agreement shall terminate one (1) year from the Effective D ate.
perturbation.explanation: Reduced the agreement's termination period from ten years to one year, which may not provide adequate protection for trade secrets, potentially conflicting with state laws regarding trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: By reducing the term of the NDA to one year, the modified text potentially undermines the 'reasonable efforts' requirement of Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90(2)(a). If the confidential information retains its value and secrecy beyond this one-year period, the shorter NDA duration may be insufficient to protect it as a trade secret, making it more vulnerable to misappropriation without legal recourse.

(b) Disclosing or using without express or implied consent a trade secret of another if the person did any of the following: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the agreement's term from ten years to one year. The Law Explanation states this potentially undermines the 'reasonable efforts' requirement of Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90(2)(a) to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2, from Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90, defines 'trade secret' and outlines misappropriation. Specifically, it states information must be the 'subject of efforts to maintain its secrecy that are reasonable under the circumstances.' Reducing the NDA term to one year may not be considered a 'reasonable effort' to maintain secrecy, especially if the trade secret's value extends beyond that period, directly contradicting the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy under the statute.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Survival . Termination of this Agreement for any reason shall not relieve either party of the obligations of confidentiality and non- use respecting Confidential Information disclosed to such party , which shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of ten (10) years, or i n the case of a trade secret, such Confidential Information shall survive until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret .
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Survival . Termination of this Agreement for any reason shall not relieve either party of the obligations of confidentiality and non- use respecting Confidential Information disclosed to such party , which shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of two (2) years, or i n the case of a trade secret, such Confidential Information shall survive until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret .
perturbation.explanation: Reduced the survival period for confidentiality obligations from ten years to two years, which might be insufficient to protect certain types of confidential information, particularly trade secrets with a longer useful life, thus conflicting with state trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the survival period for confidentiality to two years could be seen as a failure to maintain reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets as required by Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90(2)(a). If the trade secret retains its economic value beyond this period, the limited survival clause may not provide adequate protection, potentially jeopardizing its trade secret status and remedies against misappropriation.

(b) Disclosing or using without express or implied consent a trade secret of another if the person did any of the following: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the survival period for confidentiality obligations from ten years to two years, potentially conflicting with Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90(2)(a) which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically WI Stat § 134.90(2)(c)2, states that a trade secret requires that "The information is the subject of efforts to maintain its secrecy that are reasonable under the circumstances." A two-year survival period might not be considered reasonable for trade secrets with a longer economic value, thus contradicting the requirement to maintain reasonable efforts to protect the trade secret. The original text provided a ten-year period, which is presumably more reasonable.

perturbation.original_text: 1. Confidentiality Obligations . Each party shall maintain in confidence Confidential Information obtained from the other and use such Confidential Information only for the P urpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Confidentiality Obligations . Each party shall maintain in confidence Confidential Information obtained from the other and use such Confidential Information only for the P urpose. However, the receiving party has the right to reverse engineer any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause that allows reverse engineering of confidential information directly contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and potentially violates trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90(2)(b)1 defines misappropriation as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent by a person who knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. Allowing reverse engineering explicitly permits a violation of the duty to maintain secrecy that the NDA aims to create, directly contradicting trade secret law.

Previous Next 134.90 Uniform trade secrets act. (1) Definitions. In this section: (a) “Improper means" includes espionage, theft, bribery, misrepresentation and breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy. (b) “Readily ascertainable" information does not include information accessible through a license agreement or by an employee under a confidentiality agreement with his or her employer. (c) “Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process to which all of the following apply: 1. The information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. 2. The information is the subject of efforts to maintain its secrecy that are reasonable under the circumstances. (2) Misappropriation. No person, including the state, may misappropriate or threaten to misappropriate a trade secret by doing any of the following: (a) Acquiring the trade secret of another by means which the person knows or has reason to know constitute improper means. (b) Disclosing or using without express or implied consent a trade secret of another if the person did any of the following: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a clause that allows reverse engineering of confidential information, which directly contradicts the purpose of an NDA and violates trade secret laws. Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90(2)(b)1 defines misappropriation as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. By allowing reverse engineering, the modified contract permits a violation of the duty to maintain secrecy, which the NDA intends to establish. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that "No person, including the state, may misappropriate or threaten to misappropriate a trade secret by doing any of the following: (b) Disclosing or using without express or implied consent a trade secret of another". This clearly shows that reverse engineering, which involves using a trade secret without consent, constitutes misappropriation, creating a direct legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Compelled Disclosure. If any party becomes legally co mpelled to disclose Confidential Information of the other, the party so compelled shall promptly notify the other party and shall cooperate with the other party in securing a protective order or any similar action taken to maintain the confidentiality of s uch Confidential Information. The party so compelled shall disclose only that portion of the Confidential Information which it is legally required to furnish.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Compelled Disclosure. If any party becomes legally co mpelled to disclose Confidential Information of the other, the party so compelled is not required to notify the other party or cooperate in securing a protective order. The party so compelled may disclose all Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to notify and cooperate when compelled to disclose confidential information, and allowing full disclosure, directly contradicts efforts to protect trade secrets and potentially violates legal duties to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the obligation to notify the other party and cooperate in securing a protective order, the altered NDA directly conflicts with the implied duty to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets as outlined in Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90(2)(b)1. Without such notification and cooperation, the disclosing party cannot take steps to protect its trade secrets in legal proceedings, effectively enabling misappropriation by removing safeguards.

In this section: (a) “Improper means" includes espionage, theft, bribery, misrepresentation and breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy. (b) “Readily ascertainable" information does not include information accessible through a license agreement or by an employee under a confidentiality agreement with his or her employer. (c) “Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process to which all of the following apply: 1. The information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. 2. (b) Disclosing or using without express or implied consent a trade secret of another if the person did any of the following: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the obligation to notify the other party and cooperate in securing a protective order, and allows for full disclosure of confidential information. This contradicts Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90(2), which prohibits misappropriation of a trade secret. Specifically, § 134.90(1)(a) defines 'Improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy.' By removing the notification and cooperation requirements, the changed text enables the disclosing party to breach their duty to maintain secrecy, thus facilitating misappropriation of trade secrets, and undermining efforts to maintain secrecy as described in § 134.90(1)(c)2 which states 'The information is the subject of efforts to maintain its secrecy that are reasonable under the circumstances.'
